      VIEs
   =20
 =20
You can get a good sense of the differences between American and Chinese fi=
nancial regulation from the recent troubles of Chinese tech companies liste=
d in the U.S. Some Chinese companies in the internet, ride-sharing, educati=
on-technology, etc. sectors have listed in New York, and Chinese regulators=
 have  become unhappy about these companies=C2=A0and  have reacted by doing=
 things like  restricting downloads of=C2=A0those companies=E2=80=99 apps, =
or  forbidding them from making profits. This has been bad for the companie=
s=E2=80=99 stock prices, for what I hope are obvious discounted-cash-flow-r=
elated reasons.I do not have any special insight into=C2=A0why=C2=A0Chinese=
 regulators are doing these things, though there are stated reasons =E2=80=
=94=C2=A0China  worries about data security at tech companies, it worries a=
bout the social downsides of expensive high-stakes tutoring, etc. =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0that are plausible enough. And here=E2=80=99s Ray Dalio:In this case =
the policy makers signaled to DiDi that it might not be best to go ahead wi=
th the listing and they understandably want to deal with the data privacy i=
ssue. In the case of the educational tutoring companies they want to reduce=
 the educational inequality and the financial burden on those who are despe=
rate to have their children have these services but can=E2=80=99t afford th=
em by making them broadly available. They believe that these things are bet=
ter for the country even if the shareholders don=E2=80=99t like it. =E2=80=
=A6To understand what=E2=80=99s going on you need to understand that China =
is a state capitalist system which means that the state runs capitalism to =
serve the interests of most people and that policy makers won=E2=80=99t let=
 the sensitivities of those in the capital markets and rich capitalists sta=
nd in the way of doing what they believe is best for the most people of the=
 country. Rather, those in the capital markets and capitalists have to unde=
rstand their subordinate places in the system or they will suffer the conse=
quences of their mistakes. For example, they need to not mistake their havi=
ng riches for having power for determining how things will go.=C2=A0U.S. se=
curities regulators, meanwhile, are in the investor-protection business, an=
d they are unhappy that these Chinese stocks (1) were sold to U.S. investor=
s and (2)=C2=A0went down. But their main, almost only, regulatory tool is d=
isclosure. So when they see a broad swath of Chinese companies=E2=80=99 sto=
cks go down for reasons like =E2=80=9Ca whole sector is not allowed to make=
 profits anymore,=E2=80=9D they naturally think =E2=80=9Cwell this is a ris=
k that should be disclosed.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0On the one hand that is a natural=
 thing for them to think. On the other hand it is awkward because =E2=80=A6=
=C2=A0that=C2=A0was a risk that was pretty well disclosed? Like a notable f=
act about Chinese tech companies listing in the U.S. is that they all discl=
ose pretty clear risk factors to the effect of =E2=80=9Cwe are a Chinese te=
ch company, we are not actually allowed to be owned by foreign investors, w=
e=E2=80=99ve got this rickety structure to get around the rules, we think i=
t works but no one is really sure, and if it doesn=E2=80=99t work all sorts=
 of terrible things can happen and you=E2=80=99ll never get your profits.=
=E2=80=9D The rickety structure is called a =E2=80=9Cvariable interest enti=
ty,=E2=80=9D we  talked about it last month,=C2=A0and it is all pretty well=
 understood and disclosed.=C2=A0Obviously those risks are (were) ignored=C2=
=A0a lot, but that is not really a failure of disclosure.But the Securities=
 and Exchange Commission does what it does, so here is a =E2=80=9CStatement=
 on Investor Protection Related to Recent Developments in China=E2=80=9D th=
at SEC Chair Gary Gensler put out=C2=A0last week:In a number of sectors in =
China, companies are not allowed to have foreign ownership and cannot direc=
tly list on exchanges outside of China. To raise money on such exchanges, m=
any China-based operating companies are structured as Variable Interest Ent=
ities (VIEs). =E2=80=A6For U.S. investors, this arrangement creates =E2=80=
=9Cexposure=E2=80=9D to the China-based operating company, though only thro=
ugh a series of service contracts and other contracts. To be clear, though,=
 neither the investors in the shell company=E2=80=99s stock, nor the offsho=
re shell company itself, has stock ownership in the China-based operating c=
ompany. I worry that average investors may not realize that they hold stock=
 in a shell company rather than a China-based operating company.=C2=A0In li=
ght of the recent developments in China and the overall risks with the Chin=
a-based VIE structure, I have asked staff to seek certain disclosures from =
offshore issuers associated with China-based operating companies before the=
ir registration statements will be declared effective. In particular, I hav=
e asked staff to ensure that these issuers prominently and clearly disclose=
:That investors are not buying shares of a China-based operating company bu=
t instead are buying shares of a shell company issuer that maintains servic=
e agreements with the associated operating company. Thus, the business desc=
ription of the issuer should clearly distinguish the description of the she=
ll company=E2=80=99s management services from the description of the China-=
based operating company;That the China-based operating company, the shell c=
ompany issuer, and investors face uncertainty about future actions by the g=
overnment of China that could significantly affect the operating company=E2=
=80=99s financial performance and the enforceability of the contractual arr=
angements; andDetailed financial information, including quantitative metric=
s, so that investors can understand the financial relationship between the =
VIE and the issuer.But those disclosures already exist in every Chinese VIE=
 offering. I  quoted some of them last month when we talked about the initi=
al public offering of Didi Global Inc., which ran into trouble with Chinese=
 regulators almost immediately after it went public in New York. =E2=80=9CT=
here are substantial uncertainties regarding the interpretation and applica=
tion of current or future PRC laws and regulations,=E2=80=9D said Didi=E2=
=80=99s prospectus, about the VIE structure. And if anything changed, it sa=
id, =E2=80=9Cthe relevant governmental authorities would have broad discret=
ion in dealing with such violation, including, without limitation: =E2=80=
=A6=C2=A0revoking the business licenses and/or operating licenses of our PR=
C entities; =E2=80=A6 [or] requiring us to restructure our ownership struct=
ure or operations, including terminating the contractual arrangements with =
our VIEs.=E2=80=9DNow, I suppose tweaks are still possible. For instance Ge=
nsler says that =E2=80=9Cthe business description of the issuer should clea=
rly distinguish the description of the shell company=E2=80=99s management s=
ervices from the description of the China-based operating company,=E2=80=9D=
 and most Chinese VIE prospectuses arguably do not do that. =E2=80=9COur jo=
urney started on the streets of Beijing,=E2=80=9D begins the founders=E2=80=
=99 letter at the start of Didi=E2=80=99s prospectus. The business section =
begins, =E2=80=9COur mission is to make life better by transforming mobilit=
y.=E2=80=9D The prospectus=C2=A0says those things because it is describing =
a large Chinese ride-sharing company, and ride-sharing companies like to ma=
ke grandiose claims about changing the world by letting you call a car with=
 an app.But technically the prospectus was not=C2=A0selling shares of that =
ride-sharing startup to U.S. investors; it was selling shares of a shell co=
mpany with certain management contracts with that startup. =E2=80=9COur jou=
rney started in a Cayman Islands law firm,=E2=80=9D a Gensler-approved foun=
ders=E2=80=99 letter might start. =E2=80=9CWe were united by a single dream=
: Being able to sell shares in a U.S.-listed company that has economic expo=
sure to the Chinese ride-sharing business that we also happen to have found=
ed, while complying with our lawyers=E2=80=99 current interpretation of Chi=
nese laws limiting foreign ownership of certain technology businesses.=E2=
=80=9D And then the whole prospectus would be like that, constantly emphasi=
zing that you are buying shares of a Cayman Islands shell company rather th=
an the underlying Chinese business. And it would be more jarring to read th=
an the actual Didi prospectus, which understandably focuses on the operatin=
g business rather than the legal structure.And I suppose that is Gensler=E2=
=80=99s goal, to make the prospectus more=C2=A0jarring, so that people who =
read it would be less likely to invest in the Chinese companies in the firs=
t place. I do not know if that's a good goal; my assumption is that the peo=
ple who bother to read prospectuses already knew all of this and decided th=
at Chinese VIE risk was acceptable, while the people who didn=E2=80=99t kno=
w all this are not=C2=A0going to read a prospectus anyway. But it might hav=
e an effect. If the first sentence of the prospectus was =E2=80=9Cwe are a =
Cayman Islands shell company providing certain miscellaneous services to a =
Chinese tech company,=E2=80=9D you could imagine journalists saying =E2=80=
=9Ca Cayman Islands shell company that provides certain miscellaneous servi=
ces to a Chinese tech company is going public today,=E2=80=9D and you could=
 imagine retail investors hearing that and saying =E2=80=9Coh that doesn=E2=
=80=99t sound very interesting=E2=80=9D and passing.=C2=A0You could imagine=
 a stronger approach of, for instance,=C2=A0banning U.S.=C2=A0listings by C=
hinese VIE companies. (The SEC  does seem to have paused=C2=A0them until th=
e disclosure is better.) That would keep U.S. institutional investors out, =
too, so they couldn=E2=80=99t lose money if China changes its rules. But th=
at has obvious downsides (they can=E2=80=99t make money either, etc.) and=
=C2=A0is not really how the SEC operates. It=E2=80=99s just going to keep c=
ranking up the disclosure requirements to make its displeasure clearer and =
clearer. But what you do with that displeasure is up to you. The Chinese ap=
proach is rather more drastic.Elsewhere, I guess a lot of investors who lob=
bied index providers to increase the weighting of China in emerging-market =
indexes  are sad now:An indexing boom that=E2=80=99s gone on for over a dec=
ade has coincided with China=E2=80=99s emergence as the world=E2=80=99s sec=
ond-biggest economy, a confluence that=E2=80=99s driven trillions of dollar=
s into its burgeoning equity market -- often via passive funds.These produc=
ts track gauges like the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, where the weighting f=
or mainland- and Hong Kong-based stocks has doubled in 10 years to about 35=
%. Or the MSCI Asia Pacific Index, where their shares account for over a qu=
arter of the market value of all members.In other words, the passive era ha=
s put untold billions at the mercy of Beijing=E2=80=99s zeal for reform.But=
 that=E2=80=99s the point of indexing, really; you get exposure to the worl=
d=E2=80=99s markets, and everything that they (and their regulators, etc.) =
do. It would be good to have an index that included only stocks from market=
s that go up, and not ones that go down, but that=E2=80=99s hard to get.
 =20
   =20
      Short ARK
   =20
 =20
Let=E2=80=99s say that you think celebrity stock-picker Cathie Wood is bad =
at picking stocks, and you want to bet against her. Specifically you think =
that her flagship actively managed=C2=A0exchange-traded fund, the ARK Innov=
ation ETF (ticker ARKK), will go down, and you would like to make money whe=
n it does. How would you do that? One thing you could do is short all the s=
tocks that she buys. You could do that reasonably effectively: ARK is an ac=
tively managed ETF, meaning that it changes its holdings unpredictably from=
 time to time, but it does seem to do a pretty good job of disclosing its t=
rading in something like real time. And it=E2=80=99s not like she=E2=80=99s=
 in a high-frequency trading business; she=E2=80=99s betting on broad econo=
mic themes rather than minute-by-minute trading dynamics. You could probabl=
y get a reasonable inverse of her fund by just watching what she trades and=
 doing the opposite.Still it is more work than the obvious trade. The obvio=
us trade is: ARKK is an=C2=A0exchange-traded=C2=A0fund, which means you can=
 buy and sell shares of the=C2=A0fund=C2=A0on the stock exchange, so you sh=
ould just sell the fund short. Instead of betting against her by shorting t=
he stocks she buys, just short the shares of her fund. Then every time her =
fund goes down you will make money, and you never need to adjust your trade=
s or pay attention to her trades. You are automatically betting against wha=
tever she does.This is a standard nice feature of ETFs: If you want to bet =
on the ETF=E2=80=99s theme or index or manager, you can buy the ETF, but if=
 you want to bet against it you can sell the ETF short. The ETF as a produc=
t appeals to both sets of people, people who=C2=A0like=C2=A0some theme and =
people who want to bet against it. (The ETF only gets management fees from=
=C2=A0the former group=C2=A0though.) And in fact Bloomberg tells me that th=
ere are about 19.3 million shares of short interest on ARKK, or about $2.3 =
billion worth of shorts. (Versus about $22.8 billion of longs.) So lots of =
people do want to bet against Cathie Wood=E2=80=99s portfolio and stock-pic=
king skills, and do, in a straightforward way.=C2=A0Still some ETFs come in=
 two flavors, regular and inverse, where the inverse one generally goes up =
X% whenever the regular one goes down X%. And=C2=A0if you want to bet again=
st the theme of the regular one you can short it, or you can=C2=A0buy=C2=A0=
the inverse one. I suppose this is more convenient or less scary for some i=
nvestors than short selling is. There are also some nice weird synergies fo=
r the company offering the ETFs. For one thing, the longs and shorts both p=
ay management fees. Also I suppose you can write a swap on the underlying t=
hing between the long and short funds, reducing the need to actually hold (=
and short) the underlying thing.You don=E2=80=99t really see inverse funds=
=C2=A0with actively managed stock-picker ETFs, for what I think are kind of=
 obvious reasons. (If you=E2=80=99re offering that sort of ETF it=E2=80=99s=
 because you=C2=A0think you're a good stock-picker; you=E2=80=99re not indi=
fferently looking to give people exposure either way.) But  that doesn=E2=
=80=99t mean that someone=C2=A0else=C2=A0can=E2=80=99t do it:Those who thin=
k Cathie Wood=E2=80=99s hot hand is cooling may soon be able to express tha=
t view via an exchange-traded fund.The Short ARKK ETF would seek to track t=
he inverse performance of the $23 billion Ark Innovation ETF (ticker ARKK) =
-- the largest fund in Ark Investment Management=E2=80=99s lineup -- throug=
h swaps contracts, according to a filing Friday with the U.S. Securities an=
d Exchange Commission. The fund would trade under the ticker SARK and charg=
e a 0.75% operating expense, in line with ARKK=E2=80=99s fee. ...SARK would=
 be managed by Matt Tuttle, chief executive officer at Tuttle Capital Manag=
ement LLC, an issuer of thematic and actively-managed ETFs.=E2=80=9CIn sum,=
 as ARKK already represents a long exposure to a basket of unprofitable tec=
h stocks, we thought that investors should have access to the short side as=
 well,=E2=80=9D Tuttle wrote in an email. =E2=80=9CKeep in mind there are a=
 lot of non institutional investors, that cannot short stocks or ETFs or th=
ey may have trouble finding a borrow to put on the short.=E2=80=9DRude! Mos=
tly this strikes me as a clever marketing=C2=A0move; surely there are some =
people who think =E2=80=9CCathie Wood is over-hyped=E2=80=9D but who would =
not go so far as to short her fund, but maybe if someone pitches them an an=
ti-Cathie Wood fund they=E2=80=99ll bite? I dunno. Anyway here's how that w=
ill work:The Fund will enter into one or more swap agreements with major gl=
obal financial institutions for a specified period ranging from a day to mo=
re than one year whereby the Fund and the global financial institution will=
 agree to exchange the return (or differentials in rates of return) earned =
or realized on the ETF.=C2=A0If you are an ETF arbitrageur, it is nice to b=
e able to get long ARKK in many different ways. You can buy the underlying =
portfolio, or you can buy shares of the ETF, or, now, you can write a swap =
to SARK and get long ARKK that way. And then your job is to get long the ch=
eapest way and short the most expensive way, so that you are perfectly hedg=
ed and make a little profit; Cathie Wood and Matt Tuttle can argue who=E2=
=80=99s right about stocks, but the arbs make money either way.Elsewhere: =
=E2=80=9CCathie Wood Is Just a Start as  Stock Pickers Storm the ETF World.=
=E2=80=9D

      Goldman pay
   =20
 =20
I  dunno man:Goldman Sachs Group Inc. raised pay for its junior investment =
bankers, among the last of the big Wall Street banks to do so following a p=
resentation by the lender=E2=80=99s own analysts revealing their crushing w=
orkloads.First-year analysts at Goldman Sachs will now earn at least $110,0=
00, according to people familiar with the matter. Their second-year counter=
parts will be paid $125,000, the people said, asking not to be identified d=
iscussing a private matter. Pay for first year associates will go to $150,0=
00, they said.Goldman held out so long  on the theory that, as one Goldman =
person put it last month, =E2=80=9CIf you behave like that [raising salarie=
s] you simply end up with mercenaries.=E2=80=9D Goldman=E2=80=99s thing was=
, we are Goldman, we are special, you don=E2=80=99t come here to maximize y=
our first-year-analyst=E2=80=99s salary, but if you come here and perform w=
ell you=E2=80=99ll have special opportunities and get especially rich in du=
e time. We don=E2=80=99t want the people who want to maximize their first-y=
ear salaries; we want the special people. =E2=80=9CLong-term greedy.=E2=80=
=9DThat is, in the abstract, a perfectly plausible theory to have, and it w=
orks pretty well for lots of tech startups. But if you are going to have th=
at theory you have to be sure it=E2=80=99ll work for you too? Otherwise you=
 just end up sheepishly raising salaries a month later, and the clear impli=
cation is that you couldn=E2=80=99t attract enough of the special people wi=
th the lower salaries, because they don=E2=80=99t think you=E2=80=99re spec=
ial enough. Now you=E2=80=99re just looking for mercenaries.Disclosure, I u=
sed to work at Goldman, back when it was special I guess.
 =20
   =20
      Bored(om) market hypothesis
   =20
 =20
Last spring I  proposed what I  usually  called the =E2=80=9Cboredom market=
s hypothesis.=E2=80=9D As I once  put it: =E2=80=9Ca lot of individual inve=
stors buy stocks mainly because it=E2=80=99s fun, and ... the more fun stoc=
ks are, and the less fun everything else is, the more they=E2=80=99ll buy s=
tocks. In a pandemic, when people can=E2=80=99t really leave their house an=
d sports are canceled, there is a lot less fun to be had elsewhere, so trad=
ing stocks seems relatively more fun, so people buy more stocks.=E2=80=9DTh=
is was not especially rigorous or anything but, you know, look around. The =
stock market didn=E2=80=99t seem especially rigorous. Stocks were rallying =
because they went bankrupt. Much of that  worked out fine actually, and the=
 bored retail investors=C2=A0often did pretty well, but at the time it felt=
 really weird. It didn=E2=80=99t=C2=A0seem like=C2=A0they were doing rigoro=
us fundamental analysis, a lot of the time. It seemed like they would rathe=
r have been watching sports, but there were no sports, so they got their en=
tertainment by trading stocks.=C2=A0Anyway Richard Thaler won a whole Nobel=
 Prize for analyzing why stock markets do dumb things, and he agrees. Here=
=E2=80=99s an interview that he gave to a Swiss journalist published last w=
eek:Professor Thaler, what do you make of current events in the stock marke=
ts? As a pioneer in the field of behavioral finance, these must be pretty e=
xciting times.I sometimes play golf with my colleague Gene Fama, and if we =
were keeping score by what=E2=80=99s going on in the market rather than str=
okes made, I think I would be winning. Part of what seems to be going on I =
call the =E2=80=9Cbored market hypothesis,=E2=80=9D because during the pand=
emic when people were working at home, especially in the beginning, they ju=
st had nothing to do. There weren=E2=80=99t even sports on television becau=
se all the games were cancelled. So there was nothing to bet on, and many p=
eople started individual investing.=E2=80=9CAs a pioneer in the field of be=
havioral finance, these must be pretty exciting times=E2=80=9D is a very ta=
ctful way to say =E2=80=9Ceverything is pretty dumb now isn=E2=80=99t it?=
=E2=80=9D

      NFT opportunity
   =20
 =20
Here=E2=80=99s a story about a woman who made the only existing audio recor=
ding of J.D. Salinger, but who feels bad about it:The author=E2=80=99s memo=
rabilia has proved to be a profitable market: In 1999 author Joyce Maynard =
sold 14 letters Salinger wrote to the software entrepreneur Peter Norton fo=
r $156,500; five years later, 41 letters Salinger had signed sold at Christ=
ie=E2=80=99s for $185,000..But Eppes has refused to sell the tape out of gu=
ilt over how she went about getting it. =E2=80=9CIn the years after I did t=
hat, I came to regret it, terribly, terribly. I have spent many, many, many=
, many hours a day thinking about this. And, of course, it means an awful l=
ot to me,=E2=80=9D she says. =E2=80=9CSometimes, I wake up in the middle of=
 the night and I think, =E2=80=98I stole that. I stole his voice.=E2=80=99 =
You know that=E2=80=99s like stealing somebody=E2=80=99s soul, right? That =
tape is not mine to give or sell.=E2=80=9DNot long after our conversation, =
Eppes called me to let me know she=E2=80=99d updated her will. The tape now=
 will be placed, along with her body, in the crematorium.Burning a unique c=
ultural artifact? You know what that means! It=E2=80=99s the =E2=80=9Cobjec=
t-fire-token-money=E2=80=9D NFT cycle: You can light a cultural object on f=
ire, and then sell a non-fungible token =E2=80=9Cof=E2=80=9D the former obj=
ect on the blockchain. You can raise money off the twin demand for (1) Sali=
nger memorabilia and (2) blockchain nonsense, and you don=E2=80=99t even ha=
ve to let anyone hear his voice. If nobody mints a non-fungible token of th=
is recording then you=E2=80=99ll know the NFT boom is truly over.
 =20
   =20
      Good succession planning
   =20
 =20
Here is a story of, not so much a succession battle as a succession=C2=A0su=
rprise, at $1.2 billion family business Scholastic Corp., the kids=E2=80=99=
 book publisher that was an important part of my and many other people=E2=
=80=99s childhoods and that=C2=A0publishes =E2=80=9CHarry Potter.=E2=80=9D=
=C2=A0Its longtime chief executive officer, M. Richard Robinson Jr., died i=
n June and left control of the company to its chief strategy officer, Iole =
Lucchese. She has worked there for 30 years, ran the business in Canada and=
 the entertainment division, pushed the company to expand its digital initi=
atives, and was a close adviser and strategic sparring partner for Robinson=
. She was also in a longtime romantic relationship with him. He left the co=
mpany to her. Seems like a reasonable choice, putting the company in the ha=
nds of=C2=A0a person who knows it well and who has a good vision for its fu=
ture?On the other hand this means he did=C2=A0not=C2=A0leave the company to=
 his two sons. The Wall Street Journal gives them some space to make their =
case for why they should run the company instead of the 30-year veteran chi=
ef strategy officer, and it is everything you could hope for:[Younger son] =
Reece Robinson, [a 25-year-old filmmaker] who has done documentary work, sa=
id he tried to be involved with Scholastic but hasn=E2=80=99t worked there =
full time. =E2=80=9CI saw myself as an adviser,=E2=80=9D he said. =E2=80=9C=
I didn=E2=80=99t want to sacrifice my early 20s to work at a corporation.=
=E2=80=9DHis brother Ben said he operates a sawmill and workshop that produ=
ces lumber, flooring and furniture from trees in Martha=E2=80=99s Vineyard =
and lives off the land, noting in an email, =E2=80=9CI fish the fish and cu=
ll the deer.=E2=80=9D He also describes himself as a writer and =E2=80=9Cth=
e poet laureate who hasn=E2=80=99t told his story yet.=E2=80=9DHe roamed th=
e Scholastic headquarters in New York City=E2=80=99s SoHo neighborhood as a=
 child, worked at the Scholastic store as a teenager and dressed up as =E2=
=80=9CClifford=E2=80=9D the dog for a parade.In response to emailed questio=
ns, the elder son said his father =E2=80=9Cvalued my perspective in all mat=
ters, particularly regarding the trajectory of the company.=E2=80=9D He sai=
d he told his father in recent years he wanted to be more involved in the c=
ompany, including =E2=80=9Cmost explicitly my desire to be a member of the =
Board.=E2=80=9DThe younger one didn=E2=80=99t want to sacrifice his early 2=
0s working at a corporation, but now that he=E2=80=99s 25 he=E2=80=99d like=
 to run one. The older one lives off the land, but he could also use=C2=A0a=
 board seat if one is available. I love them but I=E2=80=99m glad Scholasti=
c will be run by a grown-up.

licy makers signaled to DiDi that it might not be best to go ahead with the=
 listing and they understandably want to deal with the data privacy issue. =
In the case of the educational tutoring companies they want to reduce the e=
ducational inequality and the financial burden on those who are desperate t=
o have their children have these services but can=E2=80=99t afford them by =
making them broadly available. They believe that these things are better fo=
r the country even if the shareholders don=E2=80=99t like it. =E2=80=A6</p>=
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">To understand what=E2=80=99s going on you need=
 to understand that China is a state capitalist system which means that the=
 state runs capitalism to serve the interests of most people and that polic=
y makers won=E2=80=99t let the sensitivities of those in the capital market=
s and rich capitalists stand in the way of doing what they believe is best =
for the most people of the country. Rather, those in the capital markets an=
d capitalists have to understand their subordinate places in the system or =
they will suffer the consequences of their mistakes. For example, they need=
 to not mistake their having riches for having power for determining how th=
ings will go.=C2=A0</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">U.S. secur=
ities regulators, meanwhile, are in the investor-protection business, and t=
hey are unhappy that these Chinese stocks (1) were sold to U.S. investors a=
nd (2)=C2=A0went down. But their main, almost only, regulatory tool is disc=
losure. So when they see a broad swath of Chinese companies=E2=80=99 stocks=
 go down for reasons like =E2=80=9Ca whole sector is not allowed to make pr=
ofits anymore,=E2=80=9D they naturally think =E2=80=9Cwell this is a risk t=
hat should be disclosed.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On =
the one hand that is a natural thing for them to think. On the other hand i=
t is awkward because =E2=80=A6=C2=A0that=C2=A0<em>was </em>a risk that was =
pretty well disclosed? Like a notable fact about Chinese tech companies lis=
ting in the U.S. is that they all disclose pretty clear risk factors to the=
 effect of =E2=80=9Cwe are a Chinese tech company, we are not actually allo=
wed to be owned by foreign investors, we=E2=80=99ve got this rickety struct=
ure to get around the rules, we think it works but no one is really sure, a=
nd if it doesn=E2=80=99t work all sorts of terrible things can happen and y=
ou=E2=80=99ll never get your profits.=E2=80=9D The rickety structure is cal=
led a =E2=80=9Cvariable interest entity,=E2=80=9D we <a href=3D"https://lin=
k.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/24626610.259075/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21i=
ZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMDctMDcvb3duaW5nLWNoaW5lc2UtY29tcGF=
uaWVzLWlzLWNvbXBsaWNhdGVk/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBf92348fa" itemprop=3D"St=
oryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: =
none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; bo=
rder-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> talked about it last mo=
nth</a>,=C2=A0and it is all pretty well understood and disclosed.=C2=A0Obvi=
ously those risks are (were) <em>ignored=C2=A0</em>a lot, but that is not r=
eally a failure of disclosure.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But the Secu=
rities and Exchange Commission does what it does, so here is a =E2=80=9C<a =
href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/24626610.259075/aHR0c=
HM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9uZXdzL3B1YmxpYy1zdGF0ZW1lbnQvZ2Vuc2xlci0yMDIxLTA3LTMw=
/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB292144fc" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"ite=
mscope" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-b=
ottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid;=
 background-color: #ccc;">Statement on Investor Protection Related to Recen=
t Developments in China</a>=E2=80=9D that SEC Chair Gary Gensler put out=C2=
=A0last week:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In a number of se=
ctors in China, companies are not allowed to have foreign ownership and can=
not directly list on exchanges outside of China. To raise money on such exc=
hanges, many China-based operating companies are structured as Variable Int=
erest Entities (VIEs). =E2=80=A6</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">For U.S. i=
nvestors, this arrangement creates =E2=80=9Cexposure=E2=80=9D to the China-=
based operating company, though only through a series of service contracts =
and other contracts. To be clear, though, neither the investors in the shel=
l company=E2=80=99s stock, nor the offshore shell company itself, has stock=
 ownership in the China-based operating company. I worry that average inves=
tors may not realize that they hold stock in a shell company rather than a =
China-based operating company.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In lig=
ht of the recent developments in China and the overall risks with the China=
-based VIE structure, I have asked staff to seek certain disclosures from o=
ffshore issuers associated with China-based operating companies before thei=
r registration statements will be declared effective. In particular, I have=
 asked staff to ensure that these issuers prominently and clearly disclose:=
</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">That investors are not buying shares of a =
China-based operating company but instead are buying shares of a shell comp=
any issuer that maintains service agreements with the associated operating =
company. Thus, the business description of the issuer should clearly distin=
guish the description of the shell company=E2=80=99s management services fr=
om the description of the China-based operating company;</p><p style=3D"mar=
gin: 16px 0;">That the China-based operating company, the shell company iss=
uer, and investors face uncertainty about future actions by the government =
of China that could significantly affect the operating company=E2=80=99s fi=
nancial performance and the enforceability of the contractual arrangements;=
 and</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Detailed financial information, includ=
ing quantitative metrics, so that investors can understand the financial re=
lationship between the VIE and the issuer.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"marg=
in: 16px 0;">But those disclosures already exist in every Chinese VIE offer=
ing. I <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/24626610.25=
9075/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMDctMD=
cvb3duaW5nLWNoaW5lc2UtY29tcGFuaWVzLWlzLWNvbXBsaWNhdGVk/607f07ceef4b8524a319=
a23cCf92348fa" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"colo=
r: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; bor=
der-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #c=
cc;"> quoted some of them</a> last month when we talked about the initial p=
ublic offering of Didi Global Inc., which ran into trouble with Chinese reg=
ulators almost immediately after it went public in New York. =E2=80=9CThere=
 are substantial uncertainties regarding the interpretation and application=
 of current or future PRC laws and regulations,=E2=80=9D said Didi=E2=80=99=
s prospectus, about the VIE structure. And if anything changed, it said, =
=E2=80=9Cthe relevant governmental authorities would have broad discretion =
in dealing with such violation, including, without limitation: =E2=80=A6=C2=
=A0revoking the business licenses and/or operating licenses of our PRC enti=
ties; =E2=80=A6 [or] requiring us to restructure our ownership structure or=
 operations, including terminating the contractual arrangements with our VI=
Es.=E2=80=9D</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Now, I suppose tweaks are stil=
l possible. For instance Gensler says that =E2=80=9Cthe business descriptio=
n of the issuer should clearly distinguish the description of the shell com=
pany=E2=80=99s management services from the description of the China-based =
operating company,=E2=80=9D and most Chinese VIE prospectuses arguably do n=
ot do that. =E2=80=9COur journey started on the streets of Beijing,=E2=80=
=9D begins <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/2462661=
0.259075/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9BcmNoaXZlcy9lZGdhci9kYXRhLzE3NjQ3NTcvMD=
AwMTA0NzQ2OTIxMDAxMjI3L2EyMjQzMzAwejQyNGI0Lmh0bSNiZzEwMjAxX2ZvdW5kZXJzX19sZ=
XR0ZXI/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB272bb31b" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=
=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; b=
order-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style:=
 solid; background-color: #ccc;">the founders=E2=80=99 letter</a> at the st=
art of Didi=E2=80=99s prospectus. The <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomber=
gbusiness.com/click/24626610.259075/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9BcmNoaXZlcy9=
lZGdhci9kYXRhLzE3NjQ3NTcvMDAwMTA0NzQ2OTIxMDAxMjI3L2EyMjQzMzAwejQyNGI0Lmh0bS=
NkeTEwMjAxX2J1c2luZXNz/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB8aa0528e" itemprop=3D"Story=
Link" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: non=
e !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; borde=
r-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">business section</a> begins=
, =E2=80=9COur mission is to make life better by transforming mobility.=E2=
=80=9D The prospectus=C2=A0says those things because it is describing a lar=
ge Chinese ride-sharing company, and ride-sharing companies like to make gr=
andiose claims about changing the world by letting you call a car with an a=
pp.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But technically the prospectus was not=
=C2=A0selling shares of that ride-sharing startup to U.S. investors; it was=
 selling shares of a shell company with certain management contracts with t=
hat startup. =E2=80=9COur journey started in a Cayman Islands law firm,=E2=
=80=9D a Gensler-approved founders=E2=80=99 letter might start. =E2=80=9CWe=
 were united by a single dream: Being able to sell shares in a U.S.-listed =
company that has economic exposure to the Chinese ride-sharing business tha=
t we also happen to have founded, while complying with our lawyers=E2=80=99=
 current interpretation of Chinese laws limiting foreign ownership of certa=
in technology businesses.=E2=80=9D And then the whole prospectus would be l=
ike that, constantly emphasizing that you are buying shares of a Cayman Isl=
ands shell company rather than the underlying Chinese business. And it woul=
d be more jarring to read than the actual Didi prospectus, which understand=
ably focuses on the operating business rather than the legal structure.</p>=
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And I suppose that is Gensler=E2=80=99s goal, =
to make the prospectus more=C2=A0<em>jarring</em>, so that people who read =
it would be less likely to invest in the Chinese companies in the first pla=
ce. I do not know if that's a good goal; my assumption is that the people w=
ho bother to read prospectuses already knew all of this and decided that Ch=
inese VIE risk was acceptable, while the people who didn=E2=80=99t know all=
 this are not=C2=A0going to read a prospectus anyway. But it might have an =
effect. If the first sentence of the prospectus was =E2=80=9Cwe are a Cayma=
n Islands shell company providing certain miscellaneous services to a Chine=
se tech company,=E2=80=9D you could imagine journalists saying =E2=80=9Ca C=
ayman Islands shell company that provides certain miscellaneous services to=
 a Chinese tech company is going public today,=E2=80=9D and you could imagi=
ne retail investors hearing that and saying =E2=80=9Coh that doesn=E2=80=99=
t sound very interesting=E2=80=9D and passing.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:=
 16px 0;">You could imagine a stronger approach of, for instance,=C2=A0<em>=
banning </em>U.S.=C2=A0listings by Chinese VIE companies. (The SEC <a href=
=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/24626610.259075/aHR0cHM6L=
y93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMDctMzAvc2VjLXN0b3BzLXBy=
b2Nlc3NpbmctaXBvLWZpbGluZ3MtZm9yLWNoaW5lc2UtZmlybXMtcmV1dGVycy1zYXlz/607f07=
ceef4b8524a319a23cBa1fd7dec" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope"=
 style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-w=
idth: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backgr=
ound-color: #ccc;"> does seem to have <em>paused=C2=A0</em>them</a> until t=
he disclosure is better.) That would keep U.S. institutional investors out,=
 too, so they couldn=E2=80=99t lose money if China changes its rules. But t=
hat has obvious downsides (they can=E2=80=99t make money either, etc.) and=
=C2=A0is not really how the SEC operates. It=E2=80=99s just going to keep c=
ranking up the disclosure requirements to make its displeasure clearer and =
clearer. But what you do with that displeasure is up to you. The Chinese ap=
proach is rather more drastic.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Elsewhere, I=
 guess a lot of investors who lobbied index providers to increase the weigh=
ting of China in emerging-market indexes <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloom=
bergbusiness.com/click/24626610.259075/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9u=
ZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMDctMzEvYS13ZWVrLW9mLW1heWhlbS1pbi1jaGluYS1zdG9ja3M=
tZXhwb3Nlcy1nbG9iYWwtaW5kZXgtZnVuZC1yaXNrcw/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB593785=
77" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color: #000000;=
 text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-=
color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> are s=
ad now</a>:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">An indexing boom th=
at=E2=80=99s gone on for over a decade has coincided with China=E2=80=99s e=
mergence as the world=E2=80=99s second-biggest economy, a confluence that=
=E2=80=99s driven trillions of dollars into its burgeoning equity market --=
 often via passive funds.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">These products tr=
ack gauges like the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, where the weighting for ma=
inland- and Hong Kong-based stocks has doubled in 10 years to about 35%. Or=
 the MSCI Asia Pacific Index, where their shares account for over a quarter=
 of the market value of all members.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In oth=
er words, the passive era has put untold billions at the mercy of Beijing=
=E2=80=99s zeal for reform.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Bu=
t that=E2=80=99s the point of indexing, really; you get exposure to the wor=
ld=E2=80=99s markets, and everything that they (and their regulators, etc.)=
 do. It would be good to have an index that included only stocks from marke=
ts that go up, and not ones that go down, but that=E2=80=99s hard to get.</=

As a pioneer in the field of behavioral finance, these must be pretty excit=

=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But Eppes has refused to sell the tape out of guilt ov=

ng after our conversation, Eppes called me to let me know she=E2=80=99d upd=
ated her will. The tape now will be placed, along with her body, in the cre=

tant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom=
-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">a story</a> of, not so much a succe=
ssion battle as a succession=C2=A0<em>surprise</em>, at $1.2 billion family=
 business Scholastic Corp., the kids=E2=80=99 book publisher that was an im=
portant part of my and many other people=E2=80=99s childhoods and that=C2=
=A0publishes =E2=80=9CHarry Potter.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0Its longtime chief execut=
ive officer, M. Richard Robinson Jr., died in June and left control of the =
company to its chief strategy officer, Iole Lucchese. She has worked there =
for 30 years, ran the business in Canada and the entertainment division, pu=
shed the company to expand its digital initiatives, and was a close adviser=
 and strategic sparring partner for Robinson. She was also in a longtime ro=
mantic relationship with him. He left the company to her. Seems like a reas=
onable choice, putting the company in the hands of=C2=A0a person who knows =
it well and who has a good vision for its future?</p><p style=3D"margin: 16=
px 0;">On the other hand this means he did=C2=A0<em>not=C2=A0</em>leave the=
 company to his two sons. The Wall Street Journal gives them some space to =
make their case for why they should run the company instead of the 30-year =
veteran chief strategy officer, and it is everything you could hope for:</p=
><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">[Younger son] Reece Robinson, [a =
25-year-old filmmaker] who has done documentary work, said he tried to be i=
nvolved with Scholastic but hasn=E2=80=99t worked there full time. =E2=80=
=9CI saw myself as an adviser,=E2=80=9D he said. =E2=80=9CI didn=E2=80=99t =
want to sacrifice my early 20s to work at a corporation.=E2=80=9D</p><p sty=
le=3D"margin: 16px 0;">His brother Ben said he operates a sawmill and works=
hop that produces lumber, flooring and furniture from trees in Martha=E2=80=
=99s Vineyard and lives off the land, noting in an email, =E2=80=9CI fish t=
he fish and cull the deer.=E2=80=9D He also describes himself as a writer a=
nd =E2=80=9Cthe poet laureate who hasn=E2=80=99t told his story yet.=E2=80=
=9D</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">He roamed the Scholastic headquarters i=
n New York City=E2=80=99s SoHo neighborhood as a child, worked at the Schol=
astic store as a teenager and dressed up as =E2=80=9CClifford=E2=80=9D the =
dog for a parade.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In response to emailed qu=
estions, the elder son said his father =E2=80=9Cvalued my perspective in al=
l matters, particularly regarding the trajectory of the company.=E2=80=9D H=
e said he told his father in recent years he wanted to be more involved in =
the company, including =E2=80=9Cmost explicitly my desire to be a member of=
 the Board.=E2=80=9D</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The young=
er one didn=E2=80=99t want to sacrifice his early 20s working at a corporat=
ion, but now that he=E2=80=99s 25 he=E2=80=99d like to run one. The older o=
ne lives off the land, but he could also use=C2=A0a board seat if one is av=
ailable. I love them but I=E2=80=99m glad Scholastic will be run by a grown=
-up.</p><table class=3D"header" width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"=
